a:T19cd2,<div class="specification">
<p>A community living in the water of an estuary was used to investigate how climate change may affect ecological systems. The food web in this community included phytoplankton (producers), zooplankton (consumers) and saprotrophic bacteria. Small plastic mesocosms were set up with water from the estuary containing only these three groups of organisms. The mesocosms were subjected to four different temperatures and two nutrient levels (control and nutrients added) to replicate local variations of the conditions in the estuary during springtime warming.</p>
<p>The graph shows the biomass of the community for each of the eight mesocosms at the end of the experimental period. Biomass was measured in terms of the amount of carbon present. The horizontal line indicates the initial biomass.</p>
<p style="text-align: center;"><img src="data:image/png;base64,"/></p>
<p style="text-align: center;">[Source: adapted from O’Connor, M.I., Piehler, M.F., Leech, D.M., Anton, A. and Bruno, J.F., 2009.<br/><em>PLOS Biology</em>, [e-journal] 7(9). https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pbio.1000178.]</p>
</div><div class="specification">
<p>The graph shows the mean biomass of autotrophs and heterotrophs in the eight mesocosms. The horizontal lines indicate the initial biomasses.</p>
<p style="text-align: center;"><img src="data:image/png;base64,"/></p>
<p style="text-align: center;">[Source: adapted from O’Connor, M.I., Piehler, M.F., Leech, D.M., Anton, A. and Bruno, J.F., 2009.<br/><em>PLOS Biology</em>, [e-journal] 7(9). https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pbio.1000178.]</p>
</div><div class="specification">
<p>Chlorophyll concentration was used as an estimate of the photosynthetic capacity of the community. The rate of photosynthesis and mass of chlorophyll per unit volume were measured in a mesocosm at three different temperatures.</p>
<p style="text-align: center;"><img src="data:image/png;base64,"/></p>
<p style="text-align: center;">[Source: adapted from O’Connor, M.I., Piehler, M.F., Leech, D.M., Anton, A. and Bruno, J.F., 2009.<br/><em>PLOS Biology</em>, [e-journal] 7(9). https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pbio.1000178.]</p>
</div><div class="specification">
<p>In a larger study, California grassland was exposed to elevated temperature and nitrate concentration for five years. The graph shows the total biomass production in individual and in combined treatments. Error bars denote one standard error.</p>
<p style="text-align: center;"><img src="data:image/png;base64,"/></p>
<p style="text-align: center;">[Source: adapted from Dukes, J.S., Chiariello, N.R., Cleland, E.E., Moore, L.A., Shaw, M.R., Thayer,S., Tobeck, T.,<br/>Mooney, H.A. and Field, C.B., 2005. <em>PLOS Biology</em>, 3(10), e319. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pbio.0030319.]</p>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px; padding-right: 20px;">
<p>Describe the effect of temperature on the total biomass.</p>
<div class="marks">[2]</div>
<div class="question_part_label">a.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px; padding-right: 20px;">
<p>Compare and contrast the effects of temperature on the biomass of autotrophs and heterotrophs with added nutrients.</p>
<div class="marks">[2]</div>
<div class="question_part_label">b.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px; padding-right: 20px;">
<p>Explain the effect of temperature on the rate of photosynthesis in this mesocosm.</p>
<div class="marks">[2]</div>
<div class="question_part_label">c.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px; padding-right: 20px;">
<p>Suggest reasons for the decreases in biomass of autotrophs as temperature rises, despite the increases in photosynthesis.</p>
<div class="marks">[2]</div>
<div class="question_part_label">d.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px; padding-right: 20px;">
<p>Describe the effects of temperature and nitrate concentration on biomass.</p>
<div class="marks">[3]</div>
<div class="question_part_label">e.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px; padding-right: 20px;">
<p>Suggest <strong>two</strong> abiotic factors, other than temperature and nutrient supply, that may affect the production of biomass of the grasslands.</p>
<div class="marks">[2]</div>
<div class="question_part_label">f.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px; padding-right: 20px;">
<p>The first study used mesocosms and the second study was carried out in natural grassland. Discuss the use of mesocosms as opposed to a study in a natural environment.</p>
<div class="marks">[2]</div>
<div class="question_part_label">g.</div>
</div>b:Te70,<div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>a. negative correlation/decrease (in biomass) as temperature rises in added-nutrients (mesocosms);<br/>b. little/no (significant) change in biomass as temperature increases in control (mesocosms);</p>
<div class="question_part_label">a.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>a. autotroph biomass decreases <span style="text-decoration:underline;">and</span> heterotroph biomass increases with higher temperatures;<br/>b. decrease in autotrophs is greater/larger/more than increase in heterotrophs<br/><strong>OR</strong><br/>little difference in biomass (between auto and heterotrophs) at highest temperature/27 °C;<br/>c. autotrophs show smaller and smaller gains in biomass from initial as temperature rises/WTTE;<br/>d. heterotrophs no gain in biomass at 21 °C then larger and larger gains as temperature rises;</p>
<div class="question_part_label">b.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p><em>rate of photosynthesis increases as temperature rises because</em>:<br/>a. temperature is the limiting factor for photosynthesis;<br/>b. higher temperatures increase enzyme activity;<br/>c. faster molecular motion/more molecular kinetic energy/more frequent enzyme-substrate collisions;<br/>d. Calvin cycle/light independent reactions (of photosynthesis) speed up;</p>
<div class="question_part_label">c.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p><em>biomass of autotrophs decreases as temperature rises because of</em>:<br/>a. more herbivory/grazing/feeding by (zooplankton/heterotrophs);<br/>b. higher populations/numbers/biomass of zooplankton/heterotrophs;<br/>c. more mortality/more decomposition/decay of autotrophs/phytoplankton;<br/>d. <span style="text-decoration:underline;">respiration</span> (rate higher than photosynthesis rate in autotrophs/phytoplankton);</p>
<div class="question_part_label">d.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>a. increased temperature raises biomass;<br/>b. increased nitrate raises biomass more than increased temperature;<br/>c. increased nitrate and temperature raises biomass by same amount as nitrate alone;</p>
<div class="question_part_label">e.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>a. water availability/rainfall/humidity;<br/>b. light/sunlight (intensity) / daylength;<br/>c. salinity of <span style="text-decoration:underline;">soil</span> / high/low <span style="text-decoration:underline;">soil</span> pH;<br/>d. chemical pollution/herbicides/allelopathy/parasitic weeds;</p>
<p><em>Mark the first two answers only</em>.<br/><em>Do not accept carbon dioxide or weather conditions</em>.</p>
<div class="question_part_label">f.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p><em>advantages of mesocosms/converse problems with studies in natural environments</em><br/>a. easier to manipulate/control variables/conditions / less susceptible to outside influences<br/><strong>OR</strong><br/>easier to replicate<br/><strong>OR</strong><br/>take up less space;</p>
<p><em>disadvantages of mesocosms/converse opportunities with studies in natural environments</em><br/>b. some trophic levels missing/incomplete food chains in mesocosms<br/><strong>OR</strong><br/>large animals cannot be included / ethical concerns about enclosing animals in mesocosms<br/><strong>OR</strong><br/>some variables lacking in mesocosms / doesn’t show what happens in natural ecosystems;</p>
<p><em>Allow only one mark for an advantage and one mark for a disadvantage as this is a discuss question</em>.</p>
<div class="question_part_label">g.</div>
</div>c:Te40,<div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>The word ‘total’ confused some candidates who tried to write about both the control and added-nutrients without distinguishing between them. Of those candidates who realised that ‘total’ must mean the total biomass within a mesocosm, almost all got the decrease in ‘with nutrients’ mesocosms as temperature increased, but many were not discerning enough with the control mesocosms. The differences were less than the error bars, so were clearly insignificant and should have been ignored Question 2.</p>
<div class="question_part_label">a.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>Most candidates stated that autotroph biomass decreased and heterotroph biomass increased as temperature increased, but this contrast was only given one mark. Far fewer candidates gave a second worthwhile comparison or contrast. Very few candidates realised that at all temperatures the autotrophs had gained biomass from the initial level, but the gains were less as temperature rose. Similarly, few candidates stated that the heterotrophs had not gained mass at 21°C but gained increasing amounts of biomass as the temperature rose.</p>
<div class="question_part_label">b.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>About half candidates merely stated the relationship shown in the graph, rather than actually explaining it. An explanation based on enzyme activity were expected. Performance in this question and in (d) correlated well with the overall performance of each candidate on the paper, probably because biological understanding was required. Other parts of question 1 were less well correlated, as is typical for data analysis questions.</p>
<div class="question_part_label">c.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>Enzymes do not denature at the temperatures used in this experiment. Also, the autotrophs are phytoplankton living in seawater so transpiration cannot be the cause of biomass reductions at higher temperatures. The data in previous graphs showed higher biomasses of heterotrophs at higher temperatures and thus greater rates of herbivory were the obvious explanation for reduced autotroph biomass.</p>
<div class="question_part_label">d.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>This was another question where many candidates’ answers lacked discernment. The increase in biomass with nitrate was clearly greater than that with temperature alone, but the increase with nitrate and temperature combined was not significantly different from nitrate alone. A useful way of thinking about answering questions such as this is ‘If I read out my answer to someone over the phone, would they correctly sketch the relative size of the bars without seeing the actual bar chart?’ With many candidates the answer to this would have been no.</p>
<div class="question_part_label">e.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>About half of answers given were accepted. Carbon dioxide concentration was unlikely to vary enough to affect grassland biomass production. Vague answers such as ‘pH’ were not accepted but soil pH was.</p>
<div class="question_part_label">f.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>Because this was a ‘discuss’ question, one mark was awarded for arguments in favour or mesocosms and one mark for counter arguments. The best answers weighed up the relative advantage of these two approaches, rather than just singing the praises of one of them.</p>
<div class="question_part_label">g.</div>
</div>2:["$","article",null,{"className":"q-page","children":[["$","p",null,{"style":{"margin":0},"children":["$","$L6",null,{"href":"/subject/biology/topic/4/","children":["← back to ","Biology"," topic ","4"]}]}],["$","h1",null,{"children":"21N.2.HL.TZ0.1"}],["$","div",null,{"className":"q-meta","children":[["$","span",null,{"className":"tag","children":"pestle"}],["$","span",null,{"children":"Biology"}],null,["$","span",null,{"children":"HL"}],["$","span",null,{"children":["Paper ","2"]}],["$","span",null,{"children":"21N"}],["$","span",null,{"children":["· ","4-1-species-communities-and-ecosystems, 4-2-energy-flow"]}],["$","a",null,{"href":"https://pestle.pages.dev/app/","target":"_blank","rel":"noopener","children":"source ↗"}]]}],["$","div",null,{"className":"q-content","dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"$a"}}],["$","details",null,{"className":"solution","children":[["$","summary",null,{"children":"Markscheme / solution"}],["$","div",null,{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"$b"}}]]}],["$","details",null,{"className":"solution","children":[["$","summary",null,{"children":"Examiners’ report"}],["$","div",null,{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"$c"}}]]}]]}]
